coaching.coaching: Pin down acceptance criteria completeness and verifiability with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria completeness and verifiability

coaching.coaching: Plan how partial pipeline failure recovery procedure will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: partial pipeline failure recovery procedure

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

AC item "v0.10.1 tag + GitHub Release created at the released main SHA: 'gh release view " cites operational evidence (cloud) that lives outside the codebase but is not tagged [operator-attested]; prefix the checkbox text with [operator-attested] so the completion verifier accepts a recorded attestation instead of failing to find code proof.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either mandate the commit subject in Step 5 (e.g. 'Release 0.10.1' as the first line) so the grep is guaranteed to match, or replace the git log grep with a command that checks the Gerrit change status directly (e.g. via `ssh gerrit gerrit query --format=JSON change:<n>` or `gh api` against the merge commit SHA).
evidence: Step 5 specifies trailers ('rebar-ticket: zincoid-panoramic-eeve' + DCO sign-off) but does not mandate a commit subject containing 'release 0.10.1'
AC 3 relies on this grep as part of the MERGED verification, yet the grep pattern is not anchored to any commit field the plan guarantees will contain that string
VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY item 4 (fixture invalidity): the git log grep does not actually exercise the claimed state if the subject line is 'chore: bump to 0.10.1' or similar
findings.finding: AC 3's proving command `git log origin/main --oneline | grep -i "release 0.10.1"` assumes the release commit subject contains the literal phrase 'release 0.10.1', but the plan never specifies this as the required commit subject format (Step 5 only mandates the trailer lines: rebar-ticket and DCO sign-off). The grep could return no match even when the commit is correctly merged, making the proving command unreliable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — third bullet: 'git log origin/main --oneline | grep -i "release 0.10.1"'
scenarios: Executor writes commit subject as 'Bump version to 0.10.1' or 'chore: release patch'; the merge succeeds; the grep returns exit 1 and the AC is falsely reported unmet
Alternatively, a prior commit on main happened to include '0.10.1' somewhere, causing a false positive
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Step 5: 'Use commit subject exactly: `Release 0.10.1`'. Then the AC 3 grep `git log origin/main --oneline | grep -i "^[a-f0-9]* release 0\.10\.1$"` is guaranteed to match if and only if that commit landed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the AC 1 proving command to valid shell: `[ $(grep -cF '0.10.1' pyproject.toml) -ge 1 ]` (using `-F` for fixed-string matching) and similarly `[ $(grep -cF '0.10.1' server.json) -eq 2 ]`, or rely solely on `release_guards.py version-lockstep` as the canonical check and remove the malformed shell snippet.
evidence: The string `>=1` after the grep command is not a shell test expression; the correct idiom would be `[ $(grep -c '0\.10\.1' pyproject.toml) -ge 1 ]`
The regex `0.10.1` uses unescaped dots that match any character (defect taxonomy item 1: substring false-positive)

The plan does note 'equivalently release_guards.py version-lockstep passes in the authorize job' as the authoritative check, partially mitigating this, but the stated shell command itself is malformed
findings.finding: AC 1's inline proving command `grep -c 0.10.1 pyproject.toml >=1` is not valid shell syntax (`>=1` is not a shell comparison operator for grep output) and the unescaped regex `0.10.1` matches `0X10Y1` or any string where `.` is any character, creating a potential false-positive (defect taxonomy items 1 and 2).
scenarios: An executor copy-pastes the proving command into a shell and gets a syntax error or unintended behavior, undermining in-session verifiability
A file containing '0X10Z1' somewhere in pyproject.toml would satisfy the unescaped regex
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with: `grep -cF '0.10.1' pyproject.toml  # expect ≥ 1` and `grep -cF '0.10.1' server.json  # expect exactly 2`. Using `-F` avoids regex dot-matching issues.

findings.finding: The AC asserts 'grep -c 0.10.1 server.json == 3 (top-level + 2 packages)', but the actual server.json file has exactly ONE packages[] entry (not two). A correctly bumped file would produce exactly 2 occurrences of '0.10.1', making the expected count of 3 wrong — the proving command would fail even on a valid release commit.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet: 'grep -c 0.10.1 server.json == 3 (top-level + 2 packages)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC bullet to: "'grep -c 0.10.1 server.json' == 2 (top-level + 1 packages entry)"

findings.finding: The AC verification command `grep -c 0.10.1 server.json` == 3 is incorrect: server.json currently has exactly ONE packages[] entry, so after bumping the grep count will be 2 (top-level + packages[0]) — not 3 as the plan annotates '(top-level + 2 packages)'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Version-lockstep line: `grep -c 0.10.1 server.json` == 3 (top-level + 2 packages)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC to: `grep -c 0.10.1 server.json` == 2 (top-level version + packages[0].version — there is only one packages entry in server.json).

findings.finding: The AC's version-lockstep grep count for server.json is wrong: the plan asserts `grep -c 0.10.1 server.json == 3 (top-level + 2 packages)`, but server.json currently has only ONE packages[] entry (packages[0]), so the post-bump count will be 2, not 3. The parenthetical '(top-level + 2 packages)' is factually incorrect.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC line with: `'grep -c 0.10.1 server.json' == 2 (top-level version field + packages[0].version field)`

findings.finding: The AC's proving command `grep -c 0.10.1 server.json == 3 (top-level + 2 packages)` is contradicted by the actual server.json, which contains exactly ONE packages[] entry. After bumping, `grep -c 0.10.1 server.json` will return 2, not 3 — causing the AC's proving command to appear to fail even on a correctly executed release.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet: "grep -c 0.10.1 server.json == 3 (top-level + 2 packages)"
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC bullet with: `'grep -c 0.10.1 server.json' == 2 (top-level version field + the one packages[].version entry)`

findings.finding: The AC's grep-count assertion 'grep -c 0.10.1 server.json == 3' is mechanistically wrong: server.json contains exactly ONE packages[] entry, so after the bump there will be 2 occurrences of '0.10.1' (top-level version + packages[0].version), not 3. An executor running this proof command literally will get count=2 and may falsely conclude the version bump is broken or incomplete.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet: 'grep -c 0.10.1 server.json == 3 (top-level + 2 packages)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC bullet to: "Version-lockstep holds: `grep -c 0.10.1 pyproject.toml` >=1 and `grep -c 0.10.1 server.json` == 2 (top-level + 1 packages entry) on the release commit."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a partial-pipeline recovery note to Step 6: if release.yml fails after PyPI publish succeeds (version already live), describe the manual steps to create the GitHub Release and tag at the correct SHA rather than defaulting to yank-and-bump.
evidence: 'gh workflow run release.yml --ref main -f version=0.10.1 and approve the pypi environment deployment when prompted'
'PyPI is immutable — if the published artifact is defective, yank it and ship 0.10.2' — stated only for defective artifacts, not for partial pipeline failures
No mention of what to do if the workflow fails after PyPI publish succeeds but before tag/GH Release creation
findings.finding: The plan dispatches `gh workflow run release.yml` as a black box but does not specify a recovery procedure for partial-pipeline failure: if release.yml succeeds at PyPI publish but fails before GitHub Release/tag creation, the plan's only stated fallback ('yank and ship 0.10.2') applies to defective artifacts, not to workflow interruptions. Re-running a failed workflow in this state would hit a PyPI conflict (version already published), leaving the release in an unrecoverable partial state without manual intervention that the plan does not describe.
findings.location: Plan Step 6 / Acceptance Criteria (v0.10.1 tag + GitHub Release AC)
scenarios: release.yml publish job succeeds (0.10.1 lands on PyPI) but a subsequent create-github-release job fails due to a transient API error; operator re-runs workflow and gets a PyPI 400 'File already exists'; no tag or GitHub Release exists; plan gives no recovery path
findings.suggested_fix: After the `gh workflow run` line, add: 'If the workflow fails after the PyPI publish step, do NOT re-run the full workflow (it will fail on PyPI conflict). Instead: (1) confirm the published wheel is correct with `twine check`; (2) manually push the tag: `git tag v0.10.1 <merged-sha> && git push origin v0.10.1`; (3) create the GitHub Release: `gh release create v0.10.1 --target main --title "v0.10.1" --notes-file <changelog-excerpt>`; (4) verify all ACs. Only yank if the artifact itself is defective.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
